body_txt: A Hate Sink is a character who is set up by the story to be thoroughly unbearable, with no wide range of anything (humor, relatability, competency, etc.) to make the audience interested in their successes. Plenty of characters are defined by their jerkishness or lack of sympathy , but there's something about this character who feels like a chore to have to watch and learn about. Furthering an audience's disgust usually comes down to how many angles a writer takes with characters' self-aggrandizement, before stopping at one whose suffering is practically guaranteed after years of making the audience feel sad or angry. While karma going from zero to hundred is often enjoyable to the viewers by default, since real life is hardly ever as black and white , these characters have a special place in the hell that they've wreaked for the former. These characters are, put simply, designed by the creator with the intent of being HATED, and hated in a very specific way. While they may be incredibly heinous and cruel, they don't have to be. In fact, they'll rarely be the main villain or even a major player because too much screentime with them could turn the audience away. Instead, they're unpleasant not for being evil, but for being flat-out dislikeable in terms of personality and presentation. Unlike your typical villain, they won't be entertaining to watch. They'll lack the depth necessary to humanize them. They'll be grating to sit through, without the saving graces of competence, humor, charisma, or any other quality that makes the audience enjoy their presence in spite of their actions. A good rule of thumb here is, if you can remove their villainous actions and be left with a character you'd enjoy watching for one reason or another, they're probably not a sink. The Hate Sink is fundamentally distinct from The Scrappy : the writers WROTE the Hate Sink to be hated, while the Scrappy garnered a Hatedom on their own. That said, the two aren't mutually exclusive; a Hate Sink can become a Scrappy if the audience hates them for reasons other than what the creators intended. Such as karma having its flames extinguished before they can touch the character, the audience finding it uncomfortable to stomach them as a whole due to how often they've ruined what was beforehand thoroughly set up to be a happy ending, being intentionally annoying far too well to the point that the audience feels their presence detracts from the story's enjoyment, their actions coming across as so overdone in their realistic awfulness that they practically define the tone of the work, and/or coming across as a generic villain who's lack of any distinguishing characteristics beyond their loathsomeness makes them boring and dull. However, it is important to note that what the audience thinks of the character is irrelevant to their Sink status. While rare, it is possible for a Hate Sink to end up becoming popular as long as the character in question was originally written to be hated but have amassed a large fanbase in spite of that. This can occur when either the character's negative traits are downplayed by fans or when the character in question has several Love to Hate elements that the audience widely enjoys. Likewise, not every character the audience hates automatically counts as a Sink, as such characters can either end up being hated for being poorly-written, or are compelling and/or entertaining enough that the audience enjoys watching them in spite of their loathsomeness. So to summarize: Compare Anti-Role Model , where the character is designed to be a bad example to the audience, but not necessarily designed to be hated by the audience; Love to Hate , when the viewers actually enjoy this character because of how effective they are at being hatable; The Heavy , which is about the impact for the story; Heel , the Professional Wrestling variant of this trope; Jerkass , who may be unlikable but not necessarily loathsome, especially a Jerk with a Heart of Gold ; and Jerks Are Worse Than Villains , which is when fans hate the jerkass character more than the actual villain. note The Scrappy tends to fall under this trope for all the wrong reasons. Contrast with Anti-Villain , when a villain has prominent redeeming and noble qualities; Unintentionally Sympathetic , when the audience likes the character even if the creator didn't intend them to (a character designed to be disliked is not necessarily designed to be hated); Evil Is Cool , when a villain shows cool qualities that make them entertaining for the audience; and Laughably Evil , when a villain is funny and thus entertaining. A Freudian Excuse often prevents a character from being a Hate Sink, even if the story establishes that a Freudian Excuse Is No Excuse , but exceptions do exist. If all of the work's other characters hate this individual, they are Hated by All and not necessarily a Hate Sink — it's common for both tropes to overlap, but it largely depends on whether things like Blue-and-Orange Morality are present enough for the hateability to be understandable to a normal person, i.e. the viewer.
